coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and characterization of nanocomposite material based on graphene quantum dots and lanthanum doped zirconia nanoparticles: An electrochemical sensing application towards flutamide in urine samples
coredata.dc:creator: Trinadh, Thota
Khuntia, Harisankar
Anusha, Tummala
Bhavani, Kalli Sai
Kumar, J.V. Shanmukha
Brahman, Pradeep Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Prostate cancer is one of the essential drivers of death around the globe. As a significant medication, flutamide has been utilized in the clinical treatment of prostate cancer. However, the excessive dosage and erroneous discharge of flutamide cause the meager biodegradation and make the environment risk. Thus, it is imperative to develop an electrochemical sensor for the detection of flutamide with high affectability. In this study, we report the incorporation of a lanthanum doped zirconia nanoparticles into graphene quantum dots (GQDs@La3+@ZrO2) to form nanocomposite and is used to develop a nanosensor for flutamide detection. The proposed nanocomposite was structurally, morphologically, and electrochemically characterized by FT-IR, UV–Vis, XRD, FE-SEM, EDAX, XPS, CV, and EIS respectively. Excellent electrocatalytic behavior of the proposed sensor was noticed towards flutamide reduction in 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (pH = 7). The proposed sensor showed excellent linearity within the range of 0.00175–15.75 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.00082 μM. Moreover, GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE showed outstanding stability, sensitivity, selectivity, and good reproducibility. The developed sensor was successfully used for flutamide detection in urine samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanosensor
Graphene quantum dots
Flutamide
Nanocomposite
Electrochemical sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of GQDs @La3+@ZrO2 nanocomposite modified glassy carbon electrode
Structural and morphological characterization of GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE
Electrochemical reduction behavior of FLT at GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful to Koneru Lakshmaiah Education Foundation, Vaddeswaram (A.P.) India, for providing necessary laboratory facilities. T. Anusha and K. Sai Bhavani thank Koneru Lakshmaiah Education Foundation, Vaddeswaram, Guntur, Andhra Pradesh-522502, India for providing research fellowship.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) XRD pattern of GQDs (a), La3+@ZrO2 (b) and GQDs@La3+@ZrO2 (c); B) FT-IR spectra of GQDs (a), La3+@ZrO2 (b) and GQDs@La3+@ZrO2 (c); C) UV–Visible spectra of GQDs (a), La3+@ZrO2 (b) and GQDs@La3+@ZrO2; D) FE-SEM image of La3+@ZrO2; E) FE-SEM image of GQDs@La3+@ZrO2; F) EDX spectrum of GQDs@La3+@ZrO2.

XPS spectra of GQDs@La3+@ZrO2 nanocomposites; (A) XPS survey spectrum, (B) binding energy spectrum for C1s, (C) binding energy spectrum for O1s, (D) binding energy spectrum for La3d and (E) binding energy spectrum for Zr3d.

A) CV responses of GCE (a), GQDs@GCE (b), GQDs@La3+@ZrO2 (c) in 5 mM [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− at 30 mVs−1 scan rate; B) Nyquist plot of GCE (a), GQDs@GCE (b), GQDs@La3+@ZrO2 in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−; C) CVs at different scan rate (20–200 mVs−1); D) Relationship between peak current and square root of the scan rate.

A) CVs of bare GCE (a), GQDs@GCE (b), GQDs@La3+@ZrO2 (c) in the presence of 12 μM of FLT in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) at a scan rate of 30 mV/ s; B) CVs GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE in the presence of 12 μM of FLT at different scan rates from 20 to 180 mVs−1 in 0.1 M PB S (pH 7.0); C) SWV of GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE in the presence of 12 μM of FLT containing different pH solution from 3.0 to 11.0. D) Linear plot of pH vs. potential and cathodic peak current; E) SWV obtained from different concentration of FLT from 0.00175 μM to 15.75 μM in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) at frequency = 10 Hz, step potential = −0.005 V and pulse amplitude = −0.02 V; F) linear plot diagram of obtained reduction peak current vs. different concentration of FLT.

A) Inter-assay: electrochemical signal change response of 12 μM FLT at 10 different sensors; B) intra-assay: voltammetric response at the same sensor repetitively for 10 times at a certain concentration of FLT (12 μM); C) the time stability study of the GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE sensor for detection of FLT; D) influence of various interfering agents on the electrochemical response of FLT; E) SWVs of FLT in a urine sample at different concentrations (0.0175–1.4 μM); H) Calibration curve between concentration vs peak current.
Pictorial representation of fabrication of GQDs@La3+@ZrO2@GCE.
Comparison of the proposed sensor with other reported electrodes/sensors.
Recovery of FLT in urine samples. Average of five determinations.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and characterization of nanocomposite material based on graphene quantum dots and lanthanum doped zirconia nanoparticles: An electrochemical sensing application towards flutamide in urine samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electroanalytical Lab, Department of Chemistry, Koneru Lakshmaiah Education Foundation, Vaddeswaram, Guntur, Andhra Pradesh-522502, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Electroanalytical Lab, Department of Chemistry, Koneru Lakshmaiah Education Foundation, Vaddeswaram, Guntur (A.P.)-522502, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Electroanalytical Lab, Department of Chemistry, Koneru Lakshmaiah Education Foundation, Vaddeswaram, Guntur, Andhra Pradesh-522502, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Prostate cancer is one of the essential drivers of death around the globe. As a significant medication, flutamide has been utilized in the clinical treatment of prostate cancer. However, the excessive dosage and erroneous discharge of flutamide cause the meager biodegradation and make the environment risk. Thus, it is imperative to develop an electrochemical sensor for the detection of flutamide with high affectability. In this study, we report the incorporation of a lanthanum doped zirconia nanoparticles into graphene quantum dots (GQDs@La3+@ZrO2) to form nanocomposite and is used to develop a nanosensor for flutamide detection. The proposed nanocomposite was structurally, morphologically, and electrochemically characterized by FT-IR, UV–Vis, XRD, FE-SEM, EDAX, XPS, CV, and EIS respectively. Excellent electrocatalytic behavior of the proposed sensor was noticed towards flutamide reduction in 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (pH = 7). The proposed sensor showed excellent linearity within the range of 0.00175–15.75 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.00082 μM. Moreover, GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE showed outstanding stability, sensitivity, selectivity, and good reproducibility. The developed sensor was successfully used for flutamide detection in urine samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetragonic, mutagenic and carcinogenic pharmaceuticals are a class of cytostatic or Antineoplastic agents which is restoratively utilized in cancer therapy, infections, and skin illness [1]. Among them, flutamide (FLT) is an oral non-steroidal, synthetic, and the pure anti-androgen drug being used for prostate cancer treatment [2,3]. Testosterone is a characteristic hormone which is responsible for the spread and development of prostate cancer cells in men. Flutamide slows down the improvement of prostate cancer cells and forestalling the impacts of testosterone. After human oral administration, FLT is immediately metabolized to generate around 10 metabolites, mainly 2-hydroxyflutamide and 3-trifluoromethyl-4-nitroaniline. This medication and its essential hydroxyl metabolite decline the metabolism of C-19 steroids by cytochrome P-450 framework at the target cells in the secondary sex organ. [4]. Flutamide can be excreted primarily in urine and in low amounts in feces as unchanged drug and hydroxylated metabolites. Although flutamide does not cure prostate cancer, it can expand the lifetime of the patient for a long time or years. Besides, some side effects occur because of the excessive usage of flutamide include, including diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, blood in urine, liver malfunction, breast fullness, etc. [5–9]. Therefore, the detection of antiandrogen drug FLT in biological samples holds extreme urgency.

So far, various methods have been advanced and applied to detect and quantify FLT in various samples. These particularly include high performance liquid chromatography coupled with ultraviolet [10], flow injection analysis [11] gas chromatography [12], spectrophotometry [14], chemiluminescence [13], and radioimmunoassay [15]. These methods exhibit different problems. The flow injection method has low LOD, whereas the spectroscopy and HPLC methods do not have definite LOD. Subsequently, nowadays electrochemical sensors have an extraordinary fascinating candidate for analyzing drugs, because of their points of interest of modest instrumentation, high affectability, easy operation, excellent selectivity, rapid, efficient, minimal effort, and less reagent utilization. To date, there are only a few reports available in the literature for the detection of flutamide in biological samples using electrochemical methods/sensors.

In the last two decades, scientists have paved attention to developing different nanomaterials including metal nanoparticles, carbon nanotubes, quantum dots, graphene, electroactive component confined nanoparticles for various applications, especially for sensor fabrication and catalysis. Among all nanostructures, quantum dots have been considered a new class of materials for electrochemistry applications owing to their excellent surface and electrochemical properties [16]. In the present work, we focused on the synthesis and use of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for sensor development. GQDs are defined as a type zero-dimensional (0D) material with a lateral size of less than 100 nm. The characteristics of graphene quantum dots derived from are derived from both graphene and carbon dots (CDs). GQDs have generated meritorious properties such as low cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility, simple synthesis, economical advantage, and reliable conductivity, good thermal conductivity, excellent photoluminescence, good water solubility, and chemical inertness by the effect of quantum confinement and edge effect [17–20]. In this work, we report the most beneficial bottom-up approach for the preparation of GQDs. Metal oxide nanomaterials due to excellent electrical properties over can be used to develop novel electrochemical sensors and enhance the sensitivity and selectivity of the sensor towards the target molecule. Among various transition metal oxides, zirconium oxide exhibits astounding properties, for example, thermal stability, chemical inertness, biocompatibility, an affinity for the groups containing oxygen, nontoxicity, great electrical and surface properties. Owing to these attractive properties, ZrO2 has been broadly used as a supporter and impetus in electrochemical sensors. However, during the synthesis of ZrO2 nanoparticles, these get aggregated and thereby reducing the morphological and electrochemical features [21–25]. This issue could be overcome by doping with non-metals, transition metals, or rare-earth metals to improve the synergist performance. For this purpose, lanthanum has been used as a dopant due to its low size and good electrical properties in this work [26–29]. The development of electrochemical sensors based on graphene quantum dots functionalized with various metal nanoparticles or metal oxides for the electrochemical detection of various analytes have been rarely reported. In the present work, we have selected lanthanum doped zirconia nanocomposite for the preparation of nanocomposite with GQDs. Taking in to account the properties of GQDs and La3+@ZrO2 NPs mentioned above, assembling of these nanomaterials generates excellent electro-catalytic attributes, high surface area and also produces more active sites.

In the proposed work, initially, we have prepared graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and lanthanum doped zirconia nanoparticles through a simple bottom-up and sintering approach. Then graphene quantum dots were encapsulated with lanthanum doped zirconia using the ultra-sonication method. The GQDs interact with the lanthanum doped zirconia and forms a stable GQDs@La3+@ZrO2 nanocomposite. The presence of La3+@ZrO2 exhibits excellent stability and high electrical conductivity. The GQDs@La3+@ZrO2 nanocomposite modified GCE was fabricated by drop-casting of GQDs@La3+@ZrO2 dispersion on the bare GCE and prepared novel electrochemical nanosensor was applied for the flutamide detection. The as-fabricated sensor GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE exhibited a good linear range, a very low LOD, fast response, excellent selectivity, and sensitivity towards flutamide detection. Also, the fabricated sensor was used to detect and quantify ultra-low levels of flutamide in human urine samples.

Sodium hydroxide, potassium chloride, potassium ferrocyanide, potassium ferricyanide, ethanol, zirconyl chloride, lanthanum (III) nitrate hexahydrate (La (NO3)3 .6H2O), ammonium hydroxide, citric acid (CA), and other chemicals were purchased from HI media Pvt. Ltd., Vijayawada, India. Flutamide (C11H11F3N2O3) was produced from sigma Aldrich, China. Flutamide stock solution was prepared in 90% ethanol, respectively phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH =7.0) was prepared using 1 M Na2HPO4 and KH2PO4 solutions and used as supporting electrolyte for the detection of flutamide. All chemicals used in this study were of analytical grade and used without any further purification. Millipore water was used in the preparation of all required solutions. Solutions were deoxygenated by pre-purified with nitrogen gas for 15 min before each voltammetric experiment and the experiments were carried out at room temperature. For real sample analysis, a urine sample was collected from healthy subjects.

The square wave voltammetric (SWV), cyclic voltammetric (CV), and electrochemical impedance experiments (EIS) can be carried out using a Metrohm Auto-Lab PGSTAT 204 equipped with USB electrochemical interface using NOVA software. A conventional three-electrode system employing, bare or modified glassy carbon electrode was used as a working electrode. Pt wire and Ag/Agcl (3 M KCl) were used as counter and reference electrodes respectively. Systronic 362 μpH meter is used for performing pH measurements. The microstructure and surface morphology of prepared nanocomposites were dissected utilizing field-emission electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Carl Zeiss Model: Neon-40) equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDX). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was captured from PHI 5000 VersaProbe III instrument. The presence of functional groups was examined in FT-IR using a CDTL-LAB4-EQP-006 spectrophotometer instrument. The UV-absorption spectra (200-800 nm) of the as-prepared nanocomposites were investigated by utilizing UV–visible-NIR Spectrophotometer, V-670, Jasco. The crystal structure of the as-prepared samples was performed using X-ray diffraction (XRD) studies in the range from 10 to 90°. XRD experiments were carried out using Rigaku miniflex 600 X-ray automatic diffractometer from Rigaku Corporation, Japan.

A simple bottom-up approach has been employed to synthesize GQDs as reported in the literature [30]. Single-step pyrolysis of citric acid was used to produce GQDs followed by dispersion of carbonized products into an alkaline solution. In a typical procedure, 2.0 gms citric acid was heated to 200 °C in a beaker on the heating mantle which then converted into pale yellow within a 5 min later and about 25–30 min later turned into orange color confirming the formation of GQDs. The melted dense solution of citric acid was added dropwise into 50 ml of 10 mg ml−1 NaOH solution at constant stirring. The resultant solution was then neutralized with NaOH. The GQDs aqueous solution was stored in a refrigerator.

La3+@ZrO2 was synthesized by the sintering method following the literature procedure with slight modification [31]. 20 mM of zirconyl chloride and 5% lanthanum nitrate were separately dissolved in 25 ml double-distilled water, then mix the two solutions and stir it continuously for 2 h. To the resultant mixture add 25% ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH) drop by drop unit the pH was maintained at 10. The white precipitate was formed. Then the product was separated through centrifugation and washed 3–4 times with methanol and deionized water and dried in an oven at 100 °C for 8 h. Then the resultant precipitate was ground and sintered at 600 °C for 3 h. Schematic representation of GQDs @La3+@ZrO2 formation is given in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of GQDs @La3+@ZrO2 nanocomposite modified glassy carbon electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Before use, the GCE was polished with alumina slurries and cleaned with distilled water to remove impurities from the GCE surface. 1.0 mg of GQDs and 1.0 mg of La3+@ZrO2 were dispersed in 2.0 ml of distilled water and sonicated for 30 min for better suspension. Then, 10 μl of GQDs @La3+@ZrO2 nanocomposite material was drop cast onto the pre-treated surface of GCE; subsequently dried at room temperature. By using the same procedure GCE was also modified with GQDs. Pictorial representation of sensor fabrication and electrochemical signalling is given in Scheme 2 .

Urine samples were collected from healthy human volunteers following necessary ethical guidelines and stored frozen until assay. Before the analysis, the urine sample was filtered with filter paper to remove particulate. 1.0 ml of urine sample was taken in a volumetric cell and it was filled with 39 ml phosphate buffer (pH 7.0). This solution was spiked with the known volume of the standard solution. The quantification of flutamide in the urine sample was done by the calibration method, i.e. standard addition method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological characterization of GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The formation of prepared nanocomposite and its crystalline nature was confirmed by XRD analysis. The XRD patterns of GQDs, La3+@ZrO2, and GQDs@La3+@ZrO2 are depicted in Fig. 1 . The broad diffraction peak of GQDs at 2θ value of 25.8° (0.36 nm) was indexed to the (002) plane, indicated that the carbonization of citric acid produced graphite structures which had a more compact interlayer spacing. The XRD pattern of GQDs is identical to the XRD pattern of previous literature [30,32]. XRD pattern of the La3+@ZrO2 sample showed intensive diffraction patterns at 2θ = 24.26° (110), 29.44° (111), 31.9° (111), and 34.7° (112) which are corresponding to the monoclinic ZrO2 crystal phase [JCPDS 37-1484] and introduction of lanthanum could not alter not the phase structure of ZrO2 matrix. In contrast, the diffraction peaks of La3+@ZrO2 had disappeared in the spectrum of GQDs@La3+@ZrO2 nanoparticles, and overall intensity increases which confirm the formation of GQDs@La3+@ZrO2 nanocomposite.

The chemical bonding states of GQDs, La3+@ZrO2, and GQDs@La3+@ZrO2 nanocomposites were characterized using FT-IR (Fig. 1B). The GQDs revealed the -OH stretching vibration at 3420 cm−1, –CH2 stretching vibrations at 2931 cm−1, CO stretching vibration at 1633 cm−1 and small peaks located at 1455 cm−1, 1390 cm-1, and 1055 cm−1 are related to the CH, C-OH, and CO stretching vibration implying the vibration of the hydroxyl group and carboxyl group that functionalized onto crystalline GQDs. These outcomes are suggesting that the GQDs surface contains various oxygen-containing functional groups and the better solubility of the GQDs in water [33–35]. The FT-IR spectrum of La3+@ZrO2 shows absorption bands at 3430 cm−1 and 1640 cm−1 related to physically adsorbed water and a broad adsorption band appeared at 617 cm−1 refers to the doping of La3+ within ZrO2 [32,36]. On the other hand, the combination of bands and increase in intensity observed in the spectrum GQDs@La3+@ZrO2 nanocomposite, indicating the successful formation of GQDs@La3+@ZrO2 and strong interaction between GQDs and La3+@ZrO2.

The UV–Visible absorption spectra of GQDs and GQDs@La3+@ZrO2 are shown in Fig. 1C. GQDs exhibited one adsorption band at 220 nm which ascribed to the π-π* electronic transitions of sp2 carbon atoms and another adsorption band at 343 nm is referred to as n-π* due to the attachment of oxygen functional groups at the GQD edges, implying the successful formation of GQDs [37,38]. The absorption spectra of La3+@ZrO2 produced one absorption band centered at 225 nm can be ascribed to zirconia [39]. Afterward, the encapsulation of La3+@ZrO2 into GQDs shows two absorption bands at 286 nm and 344 nm, which are ascribed to the absorption spectra of La3+@ZrO2 and GQDs and also peak intensity decreased as compared to GQDs. These findings clearly indicate the successful incorporation of La3+@ZrO2 into GQDs and also strong interaction between the La3+@ZrO2 and GQDs.

The surface morphology of the La3+@ZrO2, GQDs@La3+@ZrO2 nanocomposite was observed by field emission scanning electron microscope as shown in Fig. 1D and E. The La3+@ZrO2 consists of a rose-like structure with a smooth surface. The FE-SEM image of the GQDs@La3+@ZrO2 shows the La3+@ZrO2 NPs successfully embedded into graphene quantum dots. The chemical composition of GQDs@La3+@ZrO2 was examined by EDX Analysis. EDX spectrum reveals the presence of C, O, La, and Zr in the prepared nanocomposite as shown in Fig. 1F.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to analyze the surface chemical composition and oxidation states of prepared nanocomposites. The survey XPS spectrum of GQDs@La3+@ZrO2 is displayed in Fig. 2A, suggesting that the GQDs@La3+@ZrO2 nanocomposite mainly consists of C, O, La, and Zr elements. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s could be deconvoluted into two surface components, ascribed to C=C/C-C (283.1 eV) and CO (286.5.1 eV), respectively as shown in Fig. 2B. This confirms the sp2 hybridization of carbon and CO group on the surface of GQDs. In the case of oxygen element, O1s core level of GQDs@La3+@ZrO2 can be split into two contributions, which are shown in Fig. 2C. The peak at lower binding energy,529.7 eV, is attributed to lattice oxygen (O−2) of La3+@ZrO2, whereas the higher binding energy peak located at 534.1 eV is corresponding to the chemisorbed oxygen species, hydroxyl groups due to water adsorption and loosely bound oxygen species, respectively. The core scans La 3d in Fig. 2D shows two doublet peaks of the 3d3/2 and 3d5/2 situated at 851.8–853.8 and 834.6–837.9 eV, respectively, indicating the La3+ oxidation state with an energy gap of (16.7 eV) [40]. Fig. 2E displays the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Zr 3d, which can be deconvoluted into two peaks locating at 181.7 eV and 184.7 eV assigned to Zr 3d5/2 and Zr 3d3/2, respectively, suggesting the presence of Zr (IV) in GQDs@La3+@ZrO2 nanocomposite [41].

CV and EIS techniques have been used to characterize the proposed material in terms of charge transfer kinetics. For this purpose, the CVs of GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE and GCE and GCE/GQDs as control electrodes were recorded in 5 mM [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− redox probe solution containing 0.1 M KCl at 30 mVs−1 scan rates between −0.2 V and 0.6 V (Fig. 3 ). All electrodes exhibited redox peaks for [Fe (CN)6]3−/4– couple. However, GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE sensor showed a well-defined CV peak in terms of high current and low peak to peak separation as compared to bare GCE and GQDs/GCE, which could be ascribed to the high surface area and more number of active sites present over the surface of the sensor. This excellent behavior of the sensor could be due to the presence of La3+@ZrO2 NPs in the nanocomposite matrix and synergistic impacts between the nanoparticles and GQDs.

EIS measurements were additionally performed to study the charge transferability of various modified electrodes. The semicircle diameter of the Nyquist plots at high frequency is related to the charge-transfer limited process and the diameter is equivalent to electron transfer resistance (Ret). Fig. 3B shows the Nyquist plots of bare GCE, GQDs/GCE, and GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE in 5 mM [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− solution containing 0.1 M KCl in the frequency range from 0.1–10 kHz. The smaller semicircle portion at higher frequencies of GQDs/GCE than the bare GCE demonstrates that GQDs can enhance the electron transfer rate. The explanation may be credited to the good electrical conductivity of GQDs. However, for GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE, the semi-circle diameter was diminished remarkably, which recommends that the blend of La3+@ZrO2 with GQDs incredibly improves the electron transfer performance and together they framed a novel nanomaterial with quick electron transfer properties.

The effect of different sweep rates on the electrochemical behavior of (Fe (CN)6)3−/4− at GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE was recorded using CV as shown in Fig. 3C. When increasing the scan rate ranging from 20 to 200 mV/s, the redox peak current and peak potential increased linearly. A linear correlation was obtained between the redox peak current (Ip) and the square root of the scan rate (υ1/2), indicating that the overall electrochemical redox process in the ferricyanide system is quasi reversible and diffusion-controlled process. The regression equations are Ipa (μA) = (20.953 ± 0.50) (υ1/2) − (2.9889 ± 0.02) (R2 = 0.9952) and Ipc (μA) = (−16.021 ± 0.45) (υ1/2) − (15.75 ± 0.025) (R2 = 0.9984) (Fig. 3D).
The electroactive surface area of all fabricated electrodes was studied by CV in the above probe solution according to the Randles–Sevcik equation [42].

where Ip is the peak current, n is the number of transferred electrons, A is the electroactive surface area, D is the diffusion coefficient, υ is the scan rate and C0 is the concentration of [Fe (CN)6]3−/4–. The assessed electroactive surface area values were 0.22, 0.26 and 0.34 cm2 for bare GCE, GQDs/GCE and GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE, respectively. These outcomes show a noteworthy improvement in the surface area after the modification with both GQDs and La3+@ZrO2 NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical reduction behavior of FLT at GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: To probe the electrochemical activities of the bare GCE, GQDs/GCE, and GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE, CV was performed in the presence of 12 μM flutamide containing 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) as supporting electrolyte at a sweep rate of 30 mVs−1 and the potential was applied in the range from 0.0 to −1.2 V as shown in the Fig. 4 . Bare GCE exhibited a minor reduction peak, which suggested the slow kinetic electron transfer. However, the GCE modified with GQDs showed improved response with lower reduction potential towards the FLT. Remarkably, the GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE displayed a higher reduction peak and better reduction peak potential compared to bare GCE and GQDs/GCE for flutamide detection. This is due to the reduction of –NO2 group to aryl hydroxylamine results in four-electron and proton (4e−, 4H+) transfer process. The change in peak towards less negative potential ascribed to the higher synergistic action. Therefore, GQDs, La3+@ZrO2 components in the prepared nanocomposite demonstrated a synergistic effect on improving the electrochemical activity towards flutamide detection.

In order to further evaluate the electrochemical behavior of flutamide at GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE, the influence of different sweep rates was examined. Fig. 4B shows the CVs observed for electrochemical reduction of flutamide on GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE with various scan rates from 20 to 200 mVs−1 in 0.1 M phosphate buffer containing 12 μM flutamide. It can be clearly observed that the cathodic peak current increases linearly with the increase of scan rate. A linear relationship was obtained between the cathodic peak current and the scan rate with a linear regression of flutamide, Ipc (μA) = (−0.2334 ± 0.20) x + (0.3058 ± 0.01) (R2 = 0.9952) (Inset Fig. 4B). The obtained result indicated that the reduction of flutamide at the GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE modified electrode was a surface controlled process.

The electrochemical process should be impacted by changing pH values because of the involvement of protons at the overall electrode surface. pH was a fundamental factor influencing the performance of the GQDs@La3+@ZrO2 modified GCE in the reduction of flutamide. In this manner, the impact of pH on the electrochemical reduction response of 12 μM flutamide in 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution at the GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE modified electrode, examined by SWV in the pH range between 3 and 11 at scan rate 50 mV/s. It was clear from Fig. 4C that the cathodic peak current of the flutamide increased up to pH = 7 and afterward gradually diminishes. Furthermore, the calibration plot of the cathodic peak current versus various pH values appears in Fig. 4D, the maximum cathodic peak current for flutamide was acquired when the buffered pH was 7. Accordingly, pH 7 was selected for further electrochemical examinations. Moreover, the cathodic peak potential was moved more negative amounts along with an increasing the various pH values from 3 to 11, which proposes the direct involvement of hydrogen ions in the reduction process on the modified electrode surface. The linear graph was obtained between cathodic peak potential (Ep) and pH with linear regression equation of Epc (V) = (−0.01 ± 0.3) pH − (0.57 ± 0.2) and the correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.995 as shown in Fig. 4D. These outcomes show an equal number of protons and electrons involved in the electrode reaction.

In order to quantitatively evaluate the affectability and linear range of the electrochemical sensor, under the optimum conditions, the SWV was used to analyze the electrochemical performance of GQDs@La3+@ZrO2 modified GCE towards the flutamide detection as it is a fast and sensitive method as compared to DPV and omit adsorption on the electrode surface. Fig. 4E shows square wave voltammograms of the proposed sensor in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.0) containing different additions of flutamide. The SWV reduction peak current gradually escalated with the increase of different concentrations of flutamide in the range from 0.00175 μM–15.75 μM, thereby demonstrating that GQDs@La3+@ZrO2 modified GCE was able to catalyze flutamide proficiently. The calibration graph of the reduction peak current against flutamide concentrations (Fig. 4F) had two linear ranges [1]. The first linear relationship was obtained from 0.00175–0.7 μM with a linear regression equation of Ipc (μA) = (9.1469 ± 0.01) + (2.742 ± 0.3) (R2 = 0.983) and the second linear range was 1.4–15.75 μM with a linear regression equation of Ipc (μA) = (0.5603 ± 0.11) + (10.234 ± 0.01) (R2 = 0.9968). As per signal to noise (S/N) = 3, the limit of detection was found to be 0.00082 μM. In this work, the proposed electrochemical sensor achieved a lower detection limit and a good linear concentration range for flutamide detection which is comparable to previously reported articles (Table 1 ). The enhanced electrochemical performance of GQDs@La3+@ZrO2 modified GCE towards flutamide sensing is credited to high electron conductivity, fast electron/proton transfer, enormous surface area, synergetic effects, more active sites, and strong interaction between the electrode and flutamide. It is evident that this GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE has excellent electrochemical activity and can be a promising sensor for flutamide sensing.

Prior to the determination of real samples, the repeatability, reproducibility, and stability were examined aiming to validate the accuracy and practicability of the proposed GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE. The reproducibility of the proposed sensor towards detecting of flutamide was studied through the performance of ten independent GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE modified electrodes prepared under identical conditions and were effectively utilized in the assurance of 12 μM flutamide at 0.1 M phosphate buffer of 7.0 pH and their corresponding square wave voltammograms were recorded (Fig. 5 ). The proposed sensor showed a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.59% demonstrating that the fabrication of the electrode is highly reproducible. Next, the repeatability of the as-fabricated sensor was examined over five repeated detections of 12 μM flutamide as appeared in Fig. 5B. The GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE displayed good repeatability with the RSD of 3.4% (n = 5) and the composite electrode does not undergo surface fouling. Furthermore, the storage stability of the GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE was investigated up to 7 weeks, and the fabricated electrode was simply stored in dry conditions without special protection. The corresponding peak current was noticed under the same conditions of flutamide every week. The peak current retained 90% of its original response after 7 weeks of storage, showing its long service and excellent stability (Fig. 5C). The above outcomes divulge the excellent reproducibility, repeatability, and long-term stability of the GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE.

Some biological interfering agents are electroactive and may influence the analytical performance of the sensor so therefore the selectivity and performance of the designed sensor were assessed by introducing some common organic compounds, for example, glucose, uric acid, dopamine, caffeine, and inorganic compounds like Na+, K+, I−, Cl−. 5 folds of caffeine, glucose, uric acid, and dopamine, 8 folds of Na+, K+, I−, Cl− were added in 0.1 M phosphate buffer containing 12 μM flutamide to evaluate the selectivity. Fig. 5D shows the current response of flutamide in the presence of interferents. In spite of the fact that an excessive amount of the interferents were added for the recognition and the current response was majorly only over FLT. It was clear that interfering agents had no critical influence on the detection of flutamide. Based on the above experimental results, the GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE sensor showed excellent sensitivity towards flutamide detection and it could be applied for the detection of flutamide in urine samples.

In order to examine the practical applicability of the novel GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE sensor, flutamide was analyzed from diluted human urine samples in 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH = 7) as a supporting electrolyte by using the SWV technique via standard addition method. First of all, the urine sample was pre-treated as described in the Experimental section for the detection of flutamide. To analyze, 1.0 ml of pre-treated urine sample was added to 39 ml phosphate buffer solution (0.1 M) at pH = 7 in the electrochemical cell and SWVs were recorded (Fig. 5E). There is no significant reduction peak of FLT was noticed in inspecting the human urine sample analysis. After that urine was spiked with known concentrations of flutamide in the concentration range from 0.0175–1.4 μM. A linear curve between the obtained peak current and concentration of FLT was as shown in Fig. 5F. The recoveries [47] were obtained in the range of 97.3–99.5% with the newly designed sensor as summarized in Table 2 . The above experimental results indicated that the GQDs@La3+@ZrO2/GCE is efficient and applicable for the sensing of flutamide in real samples.

In this work, we have developed a novel electrochemical sensor for the detection of flutamide using GQDs@La3+@ZrO2 modified GCE electrode. The mentioned nanocomposite was orchestrated, characterized, and used to improve the electrode sensitivity and electrochemical signals. XRD, FT-IR, UV–Visible, FE-SEM, and XPS studies affirmed the synthesis of GQDs@La3+@ZrO2. Moreover, the fabricated sensor displayed excellent electrochemical activities towards the flutamide because of the synergistic effects of GQDs@La3+@ZrO2 nanostructures. The proposed sensor exhibited high stability, wide linear range, reproducibility, selectivity, accuracy, and trace level LOD. In addition, the fabrication technique is extremely simple, fast, and inexpensive. These outcomes indicate that the proposed sensor has the potential for the detection of flutamide in biological samples.
Thota Trinadh: Methodology, electrochemical measurements, investigation, and synthesis of materials.
Harisankar Khuntia: Methodology, electrochemical measurements, investigation, and synthesis of materials.
Tummala Anusha: Investigation process, acquisition and characterizations of the samples, data analysis, and drafting of the paper.
Kalli Sai Bhavani: Characterizations of the samples and data analysis
J. V. Shanmukha Kumar: Visualization and supervision
Pradeep Kumar Brahman: Writing-review and editing, Conceptualization, validation and supervision

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to Koneru Lakshmaiah Education Foundation, Vaddeswaram (A.P.) India, for providing necessary laboratory facilities. T. Anusha and K. Sai Bhavani thank Koneru Lakshmaiah Education Foundation, Vaddeswaram, Guntur, Andhra Pradesh-522502, India for providing research fellowship.
